title: Built Two Unicorns in 12 Years. The Rule Has Never Changed | Glean, Arvind Jain
summary: Arvind Jain, founder of Glean, reflects on building two unicorn companies and emphasizes the importance of focus and avoiding duplication of existing work. He describes Glean as solving enterprise information discovery, valued at $7 billion after a two-year stealth period.
writeup: Arvind Jain, founder and CEO of Glean, traces the founding of the company from a productivity crisis he observed at his prior company Rubrik: as Rubrik scaled, employees could not find internal information, and per-person output metrics deteriorated sharply. He explains how Glean's core strategy deliberately avoids training its own foundation models and instead layers enterprise context on top of GPT, Claude, and Gemini, concentrating all engineering effort on the hard problem of making AI actually useful inside a company's private knowledge base. The talk covers the cold-outreach grind of early customer development, two years of free beta use that built genuine word-of-mouth, and the go-to-market inflection where Sequoia approached Glean rather than the reverse.
key_points: Glean's founding thesis came from a concrete observation at Rubrik: as the company scaled rapidly, per-person productivity metrics (code shipped, business done) all degraded, and employee surveys traced the root cause to an inability to find information and the right people internally.

Rather than training proprietary foundation models, Glean's explicit strategy is to be a fast follower on model capability, leveraging GPT-5, Claude, and Gemini as they improve, while concentrating its engineering investment on the enterprise context layer that foundation models inherently lack.

Jain ran two years of free-product beta use with 20 design partners before charging, treating paid conversion as a natural outcome of demonstrated value; one design partner's security team tried to turn Glean off and faced an internal revolt from employees who refused to lose access.

In 2022 Sequoia invested without Glean approaching them, a direct result of word-of-mouth from the free beta period, illustrating how high product quality can invert the traditional fundraising dynamic for enterprise software.

Jain frames the competitive moat not as model differentiation but as depth of enterprise integration: customers buy solutions to problems, not technology, and no foundation model provider is investing comparably in the full-stack enterprise context problem.

Cold outreach on LinkedIn for early customer feedback, before any product existed, was a formative exercise in rejection tolerance; Jain cites it as building the mental fortitude required to sustain conviction through a long, pre-revenue development cycle.
